title: Clinical Guideline for Treating Acute Respiratory Insufficiency with Invasive Ventilation and Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Updated Evidence-Based Recommendations for Choosing Modes and Setting Parameters of Mechanical Ventilation
one_line_summary: The 2025 update of the German, Austrian, and Swiss (DACH) S3 clinical practice guideline provides an evidence-based framework for invasive mechanical ventilation, aiming to standardize care and optimize patient outcomes in acute respiratory failure.
key_pearls: Avoid routine early neuromuscular blockade in moderate-to-severe ARDS, prioritizing spontaneous breathing while monitoring for patient self-inflicted lung injury (P-SILI).
Target arterial oxygen saturation (SpO2) of 92-96% (PaO2 70-90 mmHg); strictly avoid 100% SpO2 and PaO2 < 70 mmHg.
Implement lung-protective ventilation: VT ~6 mL/kg PBW, plateau pressure (Pplat) ≤ 30 cm H2O, and target driving pressure (Delta P) ≤ 14 cm H2O in ARDS.
Utilize capnography for immediate endotracheal tube confirmation and continuous monitoring of ventilation adequacy.
Consider individualized PEEP titration in moderate-to-severe ARDS using methods like decremental compliance or esophageal manometry.
Adaptive ventilation modes (ASV, NAVA) may be considered, but proportional assist ventilation (PAV/PAV+) is not recommended due to high intolerance rates.
consensus_method: GRADE framework, Evidence-to-Decision (EtD) framework, two-stage voting with >75% approval
search_period: Systematic literature search up to April 2023, with continuous integration of high-quality literature up to June 2024.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Ventilation Mode Selection & Spontaneous Breathing Integration
recommendation_blocks.narrative: When initiating invasive mechanical ventilation, clinicians must choose between fully controlled ventilation under deep sedation (with or without neuromuscular blockade) and assisted ventilation strategies that permit spontaneous breathing. Controlled modes include pressure-controlled ventilation (PCV), volume-controlled ventilation (VCV), and pressure-regulated volume control (PRVC). A Cochrane meta-analysis demonstrated no clinical superiority of PCV over VCV, and no new high-quality data have emerged to alter this assessment. The most significant paradigm shift in this update concerns the routine use of early neuromuscular blockade (NMB) in moderate-to-severe ARDS. The 2017 guideline recommended early NMB based on the ACURASYS trial, but the subsequent multi-center ROSE trial, which utilized contemporary lung-protective ventilation standards, demonstrated no survival benefit with routine NMB and revealed a higher incidence of serious cardiovascular adverse events. Prioritizing the generalizability of the ROSE trial to modern ICU practices, the panel issued a strong recommendation against routine early NMB in moderate-to-severe ARDS. Instead, the guideline strongly advocates for the early preservation of spontaneous breathing. Preserving diaphragmatic activity prevents ventilator-induced diaphragmatic dysfunction (VIDD), improves ventilation-perfusion matching, enhances venous return, and reduces sedation requirements. However, the panel acknowledges the risk of Patient Self-Inflicted Lung Injury (P-SILI), where excessive spontaneous inspiratory efforts generate high transpulmonary pressures, leading to regional overdistension and increased lung inflammation. Therefore, while spontaneous breathing is recommended, it requires meticulous bedside monitoring of respiratory mechanics and gas exchange. Biphasic, minute volume-supporting modes allow spontaneous breathing throughout both the inspiratory and expiratory phases. Meta-analyses comparing Airway Pressure Release Ventilation (APRV) to conventional modes show signals of reduced mortality (Relative Risk [RR] = 0.67; 95% CI: 0.48 to 0.94), shorter duration of mechanical ventilation (Mean Difference [MD] = 6.04 days; 95% CI: 2.12 to 9.69), shorter ICU length of stay (MD = 3.94 days; 95% CI: 1.44 to 6.45), and improved oxygenation on day 3 (Weighted Mean Difference [WMD] in PaO2/FiO2 ratio = 60.4 mmHg; 95% CI: 10.3 to 110.5). However, due to high risk of bias in small, unblinded trials, the Certainty of Evidence (CoE) is rated as very low.

recommendations.statement: Routine early neuromuscular blockade (NMB) is not recommended in patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS.
Utilize a ventilation mode that enables spontaneous breathing in all patients with acute respiratory failure.
Consider biphasic, minute volume-supporting modes (e.g., APRV/BIPAP) in acute hypoxemic respiratory failure.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: For the first time, this guideline provides formal, evidence-based recommendations on adaptive and automated ventilation modes, categorizing them into partially/fully automated closed-loop systems, neurally adjusted assist, and proportional assist modes. Partially/Fully Automated Ventilation (e.g., ASV, INTELLiVENT-ASV) utilize closed-loop algorithms to automatically adjust respiratory rate, tidal volume, and inspiratory pressure based on continuous measurements of respiratory mechanics and patient demand. Clinical trials suggest these modes can shorten the duration of mechanical ventilation and facilitate faster weaning, particularly in postoperative cohorts, but data in ARDS are limited. Neurally Adjusted Ventilatory Assist (NAVA) utilizes an esophageal catheter to measure the electrical activity of the diaphragm (EAdi), delivering pressure support in direct proportion to the EAdi signal, ensuring near-perfect patient-ventilator synchrony. Randomized trials demonstrate that NAVA significantly improves synchrony, reduces patient-ventilator asynchrony index, and increases ventilator-free days (by a mean difference of 3.42 days; 95% CI: 1.21 to 5.62 days) compared to PSV, though without mortality benefit. Proportional Assist Ventilation (PAV/PAV+) delivers inspiratory pressure in proportion to the patient's instantaneous inspiratory flow and volume. A multi-center randomized trial comparing PAV to conventional pressure support ventilation (PSV) showed no benefit in the duration of ventilation or other clinical outcomes. Crucially, PAV was associated with high rates of intolerance (up to 42% requiring a mode change) due to excessive respiratory effort, tachypnea, or hemodynamic instability.

recommendations.statement: Consider partially or fully automated ventilation modes (e.g., ASV, INTELLiVENT-ASV) in acute respiratory insufficiency.
Consider Neurally Adjusted Ventilatory Assist (NAVA) in acute respiratory insufficiency.
Do not use flow- and volume-proportional assist modes (e.g., PAV/PAV+) in acute respiratory failure due to high intolerance rates (up to 42% in clinical trials).

recommendation_blocks.narrative: PEEP is a fundamental component of lung-protective ventilation in ARDS, acting by recruiting collapsed alveoli, increasing functional residual capacity (FRC), preventing cyclic alveolar collapse and re-opening (atelectrauma), and improving ventilation-perfusion matching. However, excessive PEEP can cause alveolar overdistension, increase dead space, elevate pulmonary vascular resistance, compromise right ventricular function, and lead to barotrauma. The optimal PEEP represents a physiological compromise between alveolar recruitment and overdistension. The guideline panel conducted an extensive review of PEEP titration strategies, concluding that while higher PEEP is strongly beneficial in moderate-to-severe ARDS, no single titration method has proven superior to others in head-to-head clinical trials. Consequently, the guideline endorses a spectrum of bedside physiological titration methods as equivalent alternatives, allowing clinicians to tailor their approach based on local expertise and equipment availability. These methods include: 1. FiO2/PEEP Tables (Low-PEEP for mild ARDS, High-PEEP for moderate-to-severe ARDS). 2. Quasi-Static Pressure-Volume (P-V) Curve (PEEP set 2 mbar above the lower inflection point). 3. Decremental PEEP Trial (Global Compliance) (PEEP set at highest static compliance after recruitment). 4. Decremental PEEP Trial with Electrical Impedance Tomography (EIT) (PEEP selected to minimize regional overdistension and collapse). 5. Incremental PEEP Trial (PEEP increased until global compliance plateaus). 6. Esophageal Pressure (Pes) Monitoring (PEEP adjusted to target an end-expiratory transpulmonary pressure of approximately 0 ± 2 mbar). 7. Lung Ultrasound (LUS) (PEEP titrated to achieve the lowest reaeration score across 12 lung regions).

recommendations.statement: For mild ARDS, consider initial PEEP settings using the FiO2/Low-PEEP table.
Use higher PEEP levels in moderate-to-severe ARDS.
Perform individualized PEEP titration using bedside physiological methods (e.g., P-V curve, decremental PEEP trial, EIT, esophageal pressure, lung ultrasound) in moderate-to-severe ARDS.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Historically, oxygen therapy in the ICU was guided by a "more is better" philosophy. However, contemporary pathophysiology has highlighted the severe dangers of hyperoxia, which include the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), coronary and cerebral vasoconstriction, absorption atelectasis, and increased pulmonary inflammation. Conversely, profound hypoxemia causes tissue hypoxia, anaerobic metabolism, and organ failure. The guideline panel re-evaluated the target range for arterial oxygenation based on several recent multi-center RCTs and a comprehensive systematic review including 13 RCTs (n = 10,632). While conservative oxygen therapy (COT) generally showed minor beneficial effects (such as an increase in ventilator-free days by a mean of 0.5 days and 22 fewer serious adverse events per 1,000 patients), the panel noted a critical safety signal from the LOCO2 trial. The LOCO2 trial, which compared a highly conservative target (PaO2 55-70 mmHg, SpO2 88-92%) to a liberal target (PaO2 90-105 mmHg, SpO2 >= 96%), was terminated early due to safety concerns. The conservative arm exhibited a higher 28-day mortality rate (34.3% vs. 26.5%) and five cases of mesenteric ischemia occurred exclusively in the conservative group. To balance the risks of hyperoxic tissue damage against the catastrophic risks of profound tissue hypoxia and mesenteric ischemia, the guideline panel adapted its recommendation toward a moderately conservative, safe middle-ground target.

recommendations.statement: Target an arterial oxygen saturation (SaO2/SpO2) of 92% to 96% or a partial pressure of arterial oxygen (PaO2) between 70 and 90 mmHg (9.3 to 12 kPa) in invasively ventilated patients.
Avoid maintaining an SpO2 of 100% under supplemental oxygen, as this masks clinical deterioration and prevents early detection of worsening shunt fraction.
Maintain a strict lower PaO2 threshold of 70 mmHg to prevent subclinical tissue hypoxia.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Lung-Protective Ventilation Parameters: Tidal Volume, Plateau Pressure, and Driving Pressure
recommendation_blocks.narrative: Lung-protective ventilation (LPV) is the cornerstone of modern mechanical ventilation, with primary objectives to limit alveolar stretch (volutrauma) and high airway pressures (barotrauma). In ARDS, the volume of aerated lung tissue is severely reduced (the "baby lung" concept). Meticulous clinical trials have established that limiting tidal volume (VT) to 6 mL/kg based on Predicted Body Weight (PBW) significantly reduces mortality and systemic inflammatory markers. The Devine formula is recommended for calculating PBW. In patients without ARDS, low tidal volume ventilation (6-8 mL/kg PBW) is also recommended to prevent ventilator-associated lung injury. Plateau pressure (Pplat) represents the static pressure applied to the small airways and alveoli at the end of inspiration. Limiting Pplat to <= 30 cm H2O is strongly supported by clinical trial data to prevent alveolar rupture and barotrauma. Driving pressure (Delta P), calculated as Pplat minus PEEP, represents the ratio of tidal volume to the static compliance of the respiratory system (VT/Crs) and serves as an index of the cyclic physical stress applied to the aerated lung. Retrospective analyses of large ARDS trial databases demonstrated that Delta P is the single ventilatory variable most strongly associated with survival. An increase of 1 standard deviation (~7 cm H2O) in Delta P is associated with a significant increase in mortality (RR = 1.41; 95% CI: 1.31 to 1.51), even when VT and Pplat are within protective ranges. Sensitivity analyses support a safe driving pressure threshold of <= 14 cm H2O.

recommendations.statement: Ventilate patients with ARDS using a tidal volume (VT) of approximately 6 mL/kg Predicted Body Weight (PBW), with an acceptable range of 4 to 8 mL/kg PBW.
Ventilate patients without ARDS using a VT of 6 to 8 mL/kg PBW.
Maintain an inspiratory plateau pressure (Pplat) of <= 30 cm H2O in patients with ARDS.
Maintain an inspiratory plateau pressure (Pplat) of <= 30 cm H2O in patients without ARDS.
Target an inspiratory driving pressure (Delta P = Pplat - PEEP) of <= 14 cm H2O in patients with ARDS.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Ventilatory Timing (I:E Ratio), Respiratory Rate, and Advanced Monitoring (Capnography)
recommendation_blocks.narrative: The adjustment of the inspiratory-to-expiratory (I:E) ratio must be tailored to the patient's underlying pathophysiology. In hypoxemic respiratory failure, a moderately prolonged inspiratory time (I:E of 1:1 to 1:1.5) increases mean airway pressure, supporting alveolar recruitment and reducing shunt. Routine inverse-ratio ventilation (I:E > 1:1) is not recommended due to hemodynamic compromise. In hypercapnic respiratory failure (e.g., COPD or asthma), the primary goal is to maximize expiratory time to prevent dynamic hyperinflation. To maintain adequate minute ventilation and carbon dioxide clearance under low tidal volume settings, the respiratory rate must often be increased. However, high respiratory rates independently contribute to VILI by increasing the "mechanical power" transferred to the lung tissue. Retrospective data from the LUNG-SAFE study showed that low-frequency ventilation was associated with increased survival. The guideline panel attempted to establish a consensus on a specific RR or pH target (e.g., pH 7.25 to 7.40 with Delta P <= 14 cm H2O, Pplat <= 30 cm H2O, and VT 4-8 mL/kg), but this proposal received 73% agreement, falling just short of the 75% consensus threshold. Thus, RR must be individualized. Continuous monitoring of SpO2, ECG, and blood pressure is mandatory. Capnography measures the partial pressure of CO2 in exhaled gas. During endotracheal intubation, the presence of a persistent CO2 waveform is the gold standard for confirming correct tube placement. During ongoing mechanical ventilation, continuous capnography provides a noninvasive estimate of alveolar ventilation and early detection of circuit disconnection, airway obstruction, or acute dead-space changes.

recommendations.statement: Adjust the inspiratory-to-expiratory (I:E) ratio according to patient mechanics (e.g., 1:1 to 1:1.5 in hypoxemic failure; maximized expiratory time in hypercapnic flow limitation).
Continuously monitor SpO2, ECG, and blood pressure in invasively ventilated patients.
Use capnometry or capnography during intubation to confirm correct endotracheal tube placement.
Use capnometry or capnography to guide ventilator settings and monitor ventilation adequacy.

bedside_protocol.title: Phase 1: Intubation & Tube Confirmation
bedside_protocol.action: Immediately upon endotracheal tube insertion, connect a continuous capnography circuit (mainstream or sidestream). Confirm the presence of a persistent, rectangular CO2 waveform over at least 5 consecutive breaths. If no CO2 waveform is detected, or if only a transient, decaying capnographic signal is observed, immediately extubate the patient and resume bag-mask ventilation; assume esophageal intubation.

bedside_protocol.title: Phase 2: Initial Lung-Protective Settings
bedside_protocol.action: Calculate Predicted Body Weight (PBW) using the Devine formula (Male: 50 + 0.91 * (Height in cm - 152.4); Female: 45.5 + 0.91 * (Height in cm - 152.4)). For ARDS, set initial Tidal Volume (VT) to 6 mL/kg PBW; for non-ARDS, set to 6-8 mL/kg PBW. For mild ARDS, set PEEP according to the ARDS Network FiO2/Low-PEEP table; for moderate-to-severe ARDS, set initial PEEP to >= 10-12 cm H2O (or use the High-PEEP table). Set initial Respiratory Rate (RR) to 15-20 breaths/minute to maintain a target arterial pH of 7.30 to 7.45.

bedside_protocol.title: Phase 3: Bedside PEEP Titration (Decremental Compliance Method)
bedside_protocol.action: Perform in moderate-to-severe ARDS once hemodynamically stable, ensuring continuous arterial blood pressure monitoring and adequate sedation. If clinically indicated and tolerated, perform a brief recruitment maneuver (e.g., continuous airway pressure of 40 cm H2O for 40 seconds). Set PEEP to 24 cm H2O, maintain a constant, small VT (e.g., 6 mL/kg PBW), and decrease PEEP by 2 cm H2O every 2-3 minutes. At each step, perform a 3-second inspiratory hold to measure Pplat and a 3-second expiratory hold to measure total PEEP, then calculate static compliance (Crs = VT / [Pplat - PEEP]). Identify the PEEP level that yielded the highest Crs (lowest driving pressure) and set the clinical PEEP 2 cm H2O above this level. Immediately abort the titration if mean arterial pressure (MAP) drops below 65 mmHg, if heart rate increases by > 20%, or if SpO2 drops below 88%.

bedside_protocol.title: Phase 4: Oxygenation & Parameter Optimization
bedside_protocol.action: Adjust FiO2 to maintain SaO2/SpO2 between 92% and 96% (or PaO2 70-90 mmHg). Perform an inspiratory hold; if Pplat is > 30 cm H2O, decrease VT in steps of 0.5 mL/kg PBW (down to a minimum of 4 mL/kg PBW) to bring Pplat <= 30 cm H2O. Calculate Driving Pressure (Delta P = Pplat - PEEP); if Delta P is > 14 cm H2O, optimize PEEP titration, reduce VT further (if Pplat allows), and consider prone positioning. Allow arterial pH to fall to 7.20 (or 7.15 in severe cases), provided there is no severe right ventricular failure, metabolic acidosis, or elevated intracranial pressure.

bedside_protocol.title: Phase 5: Spontaneous Breathing Integration
bedside_protocol.action: Target light sedation (Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale [RASS] score of -1 to -2) to facilitate spontaneous breathing. Do not routinely administer continuous NMB infusions in moderate-to-severe ARDS; reserve NMB for severe patient-ventilator asynchrony, refractory hypoxemia, or when driving pressures cannot be controlled. Closely monitor the patient's spontaneous inspiratory effort for signs of Patient Self-Inflicted Lung Injury (P-SILI), such as excessive accessory muscle use, rapid shallow breathing (RR > 30 breaths/minute), or transient drops in airway pressure during inspiration. If P-SILI is suspected, increase sedation or temporarily transition to controlled ventilation.

strengths_limitations: This guideline represents a highly robust, multi-professional consensus developed under the rigorous AWMF S3 framework, utilizing the GRADE methodology and the digital MAGICapp platform for high transparency and clear traceability of evidence. The active involvement of 101 representatives from 28 scientific societies minimizes professional bias and ensures broad clinical applicability across diverse specialties. However, limitations include a lack of large-scale RCTs directly comparing early spontaneous breathing to controlled ventilation in severe ARDS, with evidence largely indirect or observational. There is also a lack of large, head-to-head RCTs demonstrating that advanced PEEP titration strategies improve patient-centered outcomes compared to simple, standardized FiO2/PEEP tables. Furthermore, most trials evaluating adaptive modes (ASV, NAVA) are small, single-center, and unblinded, often including postoperative patients rather than pure ARF/ARDS cohorts, which limits generalizability.
